data.title: BitGo Holdings stock pops 20%+ in trading debut
data.description:
BitGo Holdings (BTGO) stock jumped 25% in its opening trade as a public company on Thursday. The stock spiked as high as 36% in the first half hour of trading. By 1:30 PM ET, its gain moderated to 18% to $21.20.

The digital asset infrastructure company offered 11M shares at $18 apiece in its initial public offering, raising ~$198M in gross proceeds. Selling shareholders offered 821,595 shares in the IPO.

The IPO valued the firm at ~$2.08B.

data.title: Higher spending on subscriptions, mobile content, and hardware drives U.S. videogame sales in 2025
data.description:
Total U.S. video game sales rose 3% for the month of December from last year, while sales figures for 2025 are up 1%, according to a research report from Circana on Thursday.

December sales came in at $7.77B compared with $7.57B for the same period last year.

The bulk of sales, as usual, was generated by game content, which rose 3% to $5.93B for the month. The growth was led by a 24% jump in subscription spending and a 6% increase in PC, cloud, and non-console VR content. Spending on console and mobile content was flat year-on-year.

Hardware sales rose 6% to $1.21B for the month, while accessory sales saw a 7% decline from the prior year to $625M.

For 2025, overall U.S. sales were $60.67B vs. $59.88B in 2024. Game content sales were up 1% at $52.30B, hardware sales rose 9% to $5.44B, while accessory sales fell 7% to $2.95B.

When looking closely at hardware numbers, Circana analyst Mat Piscatella noted that Nintendo Switch 2 led the hardware market in both unit and dollar sales during December and for 2025, helping offset year-on-year declines across other platforms. PlayStation 5 (SONY) came in second across both measures and time periods.

Call of Duty: Black Ops 7 was December’s best-selling game across physical & tracked digital spending, the report said. Battlefield 6 finished as 2025’s best-selling video game in tracked full-game dollar sales. Fortnite led in total active users across both PlayStation and Xbox platforms during 2025. Roblox was 2025’s leading publisher in digital at retail spending for both December and 2025.

The month's top 10 mobile games by U.S. consumer spending are MONOPOLY GO!, Last War: Survival, Royal Match, Candy Crush Saga (NASDAQ:MSFT), Kingshot, Gossip Harbor, Clash Royale, Whiteout Survival, Royal Kingdom, and Last Z: Survival.

"Out with the old, in with the new. With the exception of Gossip Harbor, newer games are taking spots away from legacy titles," said Sam Aune, the gaming insight analyst at Sensor Tower. "With Last Z: Survival cracking the top 10, now 4 out of the top 10 mobile games by US in-app purchase revenue are part of the new wave of 4X strategy games from Chinese publishers taking over mobile gaming."

Related tickers: OTCPK:NTDOY, SONY, MSFT, TBCH, LOGI, CRSR, EA, TTWO, WBD, OTCPK:UBSFY, OTCPK:NCBDY, OTCPK:SQNNY, OTCPK:CCOEY, OTCPK:SGAMY, OTCPK:KONMY, OTCPK:NEXOY, SKLZ, SE, NTES, OTCPK:TCEHY, RBLX.

Retail stock: GME

ETFs: GAMR, ESPO, NERD

data.title: Trump's $5B lawsuit against JPM is just 10 days’ worth of its revenue
data.description:
The $5B lawsuit filed Thursday by President Donald Trump against JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and CEO Jamie Dimon represents roughly 10 days of the bank’s revenue, according to a financial analysis.

Trump alleges the nation’s largest bank terminated his accounts for political reasons, seeking damages that amount to approximately 7.1 days of JPM’s average daily revenue based on the bank’s massive financial output.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) generates approximately $507M in revenue every day, calculated from an annual net revenue of roughly $185B for 2025, according to the bank’s full-year financial results.

The institution’s operational scale is staggering – J.P. Morgan Payments processes over $10T globally in payments each day. This daily revenue figure represents an increase from 2024, when the bank generated approximately $493M per day based on total annual net revenue of roughly $180.6B.

The suit, filed in Miami-Dade County state court, alleges JPMorgan (JPM) committed trade libel and breached its implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, Fox Business reported. Trump also alleged that Dimon ran afoul of Florida’s Unfair and Deceptive Trade Practices Act.

JPMorgan denies the claims, stating accounts are not closed based on political beliefs, and the bank has indicated it will fight the suit in court.

The lawsuit comes amid a broader context. The bank said in November it faces investigations linked to Trump’s order targeting lenders accused of restricting access for political or ideological reasons, according to media reports.

Bank ETFs: (KBWB), (KBE), (FTXO)

Regional Bank ETFs: (KRE), (KBWR), (IAT), (DPST)

data.title: Banner targets mid-single-digit loan growth in 2026 as commercial payoffs persist
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Banner Corporation (BANR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Mark J. Grescovich, President and CEO, highlighted Banner's net profit available to common shareholders of $51.2 million or $1.49 per diluted share for the quarter, compared to $1.54 in the prior quarter and $1.34 in Q4 2024. He stated, “Our strategy to maintain a moderate risk profile and the investments we have made and continue to make in order to improve our operating performance have positioned the company well for the future.”
  • Banner’s core earnings power was underscored by core pretax pre-provision earnings of $255 million for 2025, up from $223.2 million in 2024. Banner's Q4 2025 revenue from core operations was $170 million, slightly above the prior quarter. The return on average assets for Q4 was 1.24%.
  • Core deposits represented 89% of total deposits, and the company increased its tangible common equity per share by 14% year-over-year. A core dividend of $0.50 per share was declared.
  • Rob Butterfield, CFO, reported, “We reported $1.49 per diluted share for the fourth quarter compared to $1.54 per diluted share for the prior quarter.” He noted that the decrease in EPS from the previous quarter was due to lower valuations on financial instruments, a loss on software asset disposal, and higher medical and IT expenses, partially offset by increased net interest income.
  • Jill Rice, Chief Credit Officer, indicated negligible loan growth for the quarter, with year-over-year portfolio loan balances increasing 3.2%. She said, “Within the commercial real estate portfolio, we reported solid growth year-over-year with investor CRE increasing 5% and owner-occupied CRE increasing 11%.”

Outlook

  • Jill Rice stated that commercial real estate payoffs are expected to continue as a headwind in 2026, but “if the economy holds up, I'm going to say we would expect to grow our loan book in the mid-single digits again over the course of this next year.”
  • Rob Butterfield described the net interest margin outlook as highly dependent on Federal Reserve actions, explaining the margin could remain flat or expand if there are no rate cuts, but could experience compression with multiple rate cuts. He stated, "If there's 125 basis point cut in a quarter or just at the end of the quarter before a quarter, then we would expect that NIM would be more of a flat scenario... If you get multiple rate cuts in a quarter, then that's where we would expect that we would see some net interest margin compression."
  • Butterfield also projected a normalized tax rate of around 19% for 2026.

Financial Results

  • Banner reported a net profit of $51.2 million or $1.49 per diluted share for Q4 2025. Full-year net income was $195.4 million or $5.64 per share, compared to $168.9 million or $4.88 per share in 2024.
  • Core revenues for Q4 were $170 million, while full-year core revenue was $661 million, an 8% increase over 2024.
  • The loan-to-deposit ratio ended the quarter at 86%. Deposits decreased by $273 million during the quarter, attributed to seasonal factors and client liquidity deployment. Core deposits remained strong at 89% of total deposits.
  • The tangible common equity ratio improved from 9.5% to 9.84%. Banner repurchased approximately 250,000 shares in the quarter and declared a quarterly dividend of $0.50 per share.
  • Net interest income rose by $2.5 million sequentially due to a 5 basis point increase in net interest margin, with the tax equivalent net interest margin at 4.03% for the quarter.
  • Noninterest income decreased due to a $1.4 million loss on asset disposal and a $2 million fair value decrease in financial instruments. Noninterest expense increased by $2.1 million, largely from medical claims, software, and legal expenses.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about loan growth outlook for 2026. Jill Rice replied that commercial real estate payoffs will remain a headwind, but the loan book is expected to grow in the mid-single digits if the economy holds.
  • Rulis also inquired about the competitive lending environment. Rice responded, “It's always been competitive... I think we compete well both in the product offering suite we have and in pricing.”
  • Rulis questioned margin expectations. Butterfield explained that the margin outlook depends on Fed actions, with potential for expansion if rates hold, but possible compression with multiple cuts.
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about deposit costs, margin, and expense run rate normalization. Butterfield provided figures for spot deposit costs and margin, and noted that higher expenses in Q4 were partly due to IT and medical claims, with expectations for 3% inflationary increases in 2026.
  • Clark asked about increases in Special Mention and substandard loans. Rice detailed that downgrades were mainly due to a couple of alcoholic beverage enterprises and idiosyncratic factors, with no broad industry concern.
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Sought updates on buyback appetite and M&A. Butterfield indicated 1.2 million shares remain under repurchase authorization, and repurchases would continue if market conditions are favorable. Grescovich said, “Our posture has not changed” regarding M&A, noting ongoing conversations.
  • Terrell and Motta (KBW) also delved into margin conservatism and tax rate expectations, with Butterfield reiterating margin sensitivity to Fed actions and a normalized tax rate of 19%.
  • Liam Coohill, Raymond James: Inquired about deposit growth drivers. Butterfield attributed growth to new client acquisition and small business focus.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, probing loan growth sustainability, margin sensitivity to Fed actions, and competitive dynamics, with a neutral to slightly positive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing strong credit metrics, stable core deposits, and robust capital levels, while acknowledging economic uncertainties and margin headwinds dependent on rate cuts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was similarly confident, but with greater focus on external rate uncertainty. Analyst questions were consistent with prior calls, remaining largely neutral but probing more deeply into margin and loan growth headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for mid-single-digit loan growth was reiterated, but management now emphasizes ongoing headwinds from commercial real estate payoffs.
  • Net interest margin guidance remains highly sensitive to Fed actions, with more explicit discussion of scenarios depending on rate cuts.
  • Deposit growth drivers and small business focus continued, though Q4 saw deposit outflows attributed to seasonality versus Q3’s strong inflows.
  • Noninterest expenses increased in Q4 on medical and IT costs, compared to Q3’s lower increases.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted slightly more toward understanding margin sustainability and expense normalization.
  • Management’s core confidence is intact, but with slightly heightened acknowledgment of headwinds and external uncertainties compared to Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing commercial real estate payoffs as a persistent headwind for loan growth in 2026.
  • Expense volatility, including IT and medical claims, contributed to higher noninterest costs in Q4.
  • Loan downgrades in Special Mention and substandard categories were mainly idiosyncratic, with no systemic industry concerns identified by management.
  • Margin compression risk remains if the Federal Reserve undertakes multiple rate cuts.

Final Takeaway

Banner Corporation closes 2025 highlighting stability in core deposits, disciplined credit management, and robust capital, while targeting mid-single-digit loan growth in 2026 amid persistent commercial real estate payoffs and margin sensitivity to Federal Reserve actions. Management remains confident in the company’s ability to navigate market uncertainty, backed by a relationship-driven approach and a strong balance sheet.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fb Financial projects mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth for 2026 while emphasizing customer focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FB Financial Corporation (FBK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chris Holmes, President and CEO, stated the company reported EPS of $1.07 and adjusted EPS of $1.16 for the quarter, with a pretax pre-provision net revenue of $71.1 million and $77.1 million on an adjusted basis. He emphasized, “Our balance sheet grew through the acquisition of Southern States Bank and was complemented by organic growth in our key businesses and geographies.” Holmes highlighted that loans held for investment grew 29% and deposits were up 25% year-over-year, attributing the results to both organic initiatives and the completed acquisition. Holmes reiterated the company’s priority for 2026: “...cement our focus on the customer. Through this simple action, we'll deepen relationships, we'll provide better products and service and we'll acquire more new associates and customers.”
  • Michael Mettee, COO & CFO, reported, “Net income on a reported basis for the quarter was $57 million or $61.5 million on an adjusted basis. For the year, we delivered net income of $122.6 million and adjusted net income of just over $200 million.” Mettee also noted a net interest margin of 3.98% for the quarter with margin expansion, and discussed the successful integration of Southern States and talent additions. He commented, “All in, our banking core noninterest expense totaled $88 million for the quarter and $298 million for the full year.”

Outlook

  • Mettee stated, “we expect our net interest margin, exclusive of loan accretion to land between 3.78% and 3.83% in the first quarter and on a full year basis, consistent with where we are today, and that assumes a rate cut baked into our forecast for 2026.”
  • The company expects loan accretion to add an additional 15 basis points, and targets upper single-digit growth in fee income for 2026.
  • Mettee reiterated guidance for full year banking expense between $325 million and $335 million, targeting an efficiency ratio in the low 50s for the full year and at 50% by year-end 2026.
  • The company is projecting mid- to high single-digit loan and core customer deposit growth for 2026.

Financial Results

  • Holmes reported net interest income of $150.6 million and a net interest margin of 3.98% for the quarter, with low credit costs and a return on average assets of 1.4% or 1.51% on an adjusted basis.
  • Loans held for investment grew 29% and deposits increased 25% year-over-year, driven by the Southern States Bank acquisition and organic growth.
  • Mettee highlighted noninterest expense at $107.6 million, including $4.6 million in merger and integration expenses, which are expected to conclude by the end of Q1 2026. Adjusted noninterest expense included $3 million in performance-based incentives and $1.2 million in franchise tax expense.
  • Provision expense for the quarter was $1.2 million due to low charge-offs. Annualized net charge-offs totaled 5 basis points, and the allowance for loan losses settled at $186 million or 1.5% of loans held for investment.
  • The company executed a stock repurchase of over 1.7 million shares, approximately 3% of the company, with its largest shareholder, the estate of the late Mr. Jim Ayres.

Q&A

  • Anya Pelshaw, Hovde Group: Asked about additional share repurchases from the Ayres Estate. Holmes responded, “we do not actually anticipate that.”
  • Pelshaw questioned the outlook for mortgage banking and platform tweaks. Mettee replied, “mortgage had a really good year ... contribution went from a negative to a nice positive number. So they're definitely on the right track,” with Holmes adding, “we're always tweaking ... but ... we're actually quite pleased with it.”
  • Pelshaw asked about the M&A climate. Holmes indicated, “a lot of conversations going on out there ... we'll continue to evaluate opportunities ... if the right one comes along, certainly, we would be in a position to act on it.”
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Inquired about loan growth and paydowns. Mettee explained elevated payoffs in Q4, and growth expectations are “not predicated on hiring new people ... with the relationship managers and bankers that we have, we would expect those to grow that high single-digit range without adding another person.” Holmes emphasized Nashville’s importance.
  • Gunther asked about expense run rate and hiring. Mettee clarified “$3 million that was performance-based related to long-term incentive... franchise tax ... $1.2 million, we should -- that shouldn't be reoccurring as well,” and added, “as opportunities come, there's an expense outlay that occurs ... we're certainly willing to do that for the right people, the right teams.” Holmes stressed, “we don’t want any of our people underpaid ... if they're not, we want to correct that.”
  • Will Jones, KBW: Asked about M&A disruption and talent/client acquisition. Holmes described integration strategies, emphasizing a “best athlete approach” to team composition. Mettee highlighted disruption-driven banker movement in Huntsville.
  • Jones asked about NIM and deposit betas. Mettee stated, “we feel good about the margin guide and the growth opportunity ... you could see both on the loan and deposit side, some margin compression if things get really ... competitive out there.”
  • Jones requested M&A criteria. Holmes detailed preferred geographies and target bank sizes, specifying, “...anywhere from a couple of billion in assets, all the way up to $6 billion or $7 billion in assets.”
  • Thomas Reid, Raymond James: Asked about loan pipeline and client sentiment. Mettee said, “pipeline is pretty strong ... we're seeing a lot of existing clients start new projects or deals ... pretty positive operating environment.” On core deposit growth, Mettee emphasized a focus on customer experience and broad-based opportunities in key geographies.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on future share repurchases, mortgage banking outlook, and expense run rates, reflecting a slightly positive but cautious tone focused on sustainability and growth drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone throughout, with Holmes stating, “I'm very bullish on the year ahead,” and Mettee emphasizing, “we really couldn't be more excited about the year ahead.” Responses to analyst questions remained measured and consistent with prepared remarks.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained consistently confident, with slightly more emphasis this quarter on the company’s readiness for organic growth and leveraging recent acquisitions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s guidance remained focused on mid- to high single-digit growth for loans and deposits, consistent with the previous quarter’s outlook, but with added clarity on achieving this with the current team and structure.
  • There was a shift toward emphasizing customer focus as the “top priority for the year ahead.”
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter continued to center around loan growth, margin sustainability, and expense discipline, similar to last quarter, but with increased focus on the implications of recent M&A activity and talent acquisition.
  • Management’s confidence in profitability and growth remained strong, with explicit targets for returns on assets and tangible common equity, similar to the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged slower organic growth in loans and deposits as a notable area of underperformance for 2025, citing economic conditions and acquisition-related distractions.
  • Elevated loan payoff activity in Q4 was highlighted as a temporary factor, with expectations to return to typical growth rates.
  • Nonrecurring expenses related to the Southern States integration, performance-based incentives, and professional services were identified, with guidance provided that these are not expected to recur.
  • Competition for talent and rising deposit costs were discussed as ongoing challenges, with management reiterating commitment to fair compensation and active recruitment.

Final Takeaway

FB Financial Corporation closed 2025 with strong profitability, successful integration of Southern States Bank, and significant balance sheet growth. Management sees 2026 as a year of opportunity, projecting mid- to high single-digit growth in loans and deposits, continued focus on customer experience, and readiness to pursue accretive M&A opportunities. Expense discipline remains a priority, with the company setting clear expectations for nonrecurring costs and targeting a more efficient operational run rate. The leadership team expresses confidence in delivering value for shareholders through a combination of organic growth, talent acquisition, and strategic capital deployment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nestlé moves ahead with sale of stake in water unit - Bloomberg
data.description:
Nestle SA (NSRGY) is moving ahead with the sale of a stake in its €5 billion ($5.8 billion) water business, which includes popular brands Perrier and S.Pellegrino. 

The Swiss food company is asking potential buyers to submit first-round bids this month, according to a  Bloomberg report on Thursday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Nestle is working with Rothschild & Co.  on the sale.

Private equity firms PAI Partners, Blackstone (BX), KKR & Co. (KKR), Bain Capital, and Clayton Dubilier & Rice have shown interest in the water business, Bloomberg has  previously reported. 

Bankers are working on debt financing of €2 billion to €3 billion to back a possible deal, according to the latest report. The debt is expected to be in the form of leveraged loans, denominated in euros and dollars. 

Nestlé declined to comment to Bloomberg.

data.title: Healthcare debate should focus on out-of-pocket costs, not list prices – Novartis CEO
data.description:
Novartis (NVS) CEO Vas Narasimhan argued that the healthcare debate in the U.S. needs to shift away from list prices and net prices toward a focus on reducing patient out-of-pocket costs.

In an interview with CNBC at Davos, Narasimhan pointed out that drugs account for only 10% of total healthcare spending and are growing at just 2%, while hospitals represent 50%, and waste along with other systemic actors like pharmacy benefit managers make up 40%.

“We really should be talking about out-of-pocket costs in the U.S. Not list prices, not net prices,” Narasimhan said. “It should be a focus on reducing patient out-of-pocket costs, which actually I think is going in the right direction.”

The CEO emphasized that the current patient landscape already provides significant protections, noting that Medicaid patients essentially have no out-of-pocket costs and Medicare patients benefit from out-of-pocket caps. Many patients without insurance also receive various forms of support, according to Narasimhan.

Narasimhan warned that the real challenge lies in addressing the massive proportion of spending allocated to hospitals, waste, and PBMs rather than targeting pharmaceutical companies.

“Otherwise, we’re going to kill the innovation engine of biotech in the U.S.,” he said.

On the international front, Narasimhan discussed the U.S. administration’s push for comparable net prices with G7 countries on a GDP-adjusted basis. He cautioned that Novartis (NVS) may be forced to delay launching new medicines in major European countries because the penalty for price disparities imposed by the U.S. government would be “just so large” and apply to the full volume of U.S. medicine sales.

The Novartis CEO criticized Europe’s approach to pharmaceutical innovation, arguing that the continent underinvests in innovation and undervalues new medicines by comparing them to the oldest generic drugs.

He stressed that pricing reform must occur country by country rather than through the European Commission, urging policymakers to accelerate the process and “re-haul how they value medicines.”

Health Care ETFs: (XLV), (VHT), (IHI), (IXJ), (IYH), (FHLC), (FXH)

Biotech ETFs: (IBB), (XBI), (FBT), (BBH), (PBE), (IDNA), (GNOM), (BIB), (HELX)

Pharmaceutical ETFs: (PJP), (XPH), (PPH)

data.title: Amalgamated aims for 10%–11% net interest income growth in 2026 while expanding political deposits and C-PACE portfolios
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amalgamated Financial Corp. (AMAL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Priscilla Sims Brown, President and CEO, highlighted a record-breaking quarter, noting "core earnings was $0.99 per diluted share, again showing the consistency of our earnings power and teeing us up to deliver consistent, growing returns on tangible common equity." She emphasized strong deposit gathering, with nearly $1 billion of new deposits, surpassing previous records even outside an election year. Political deposits increased $287 million to $1.7 billion, and all customer segments posted deposit growth.
  • Brown detailed robust loan growth, stating "loans increasing $167 million or 3.5% to $4.9 billion," and acceleration in growth-mode portfolios, including multifamily, CRE, and C&I, up 7%. The PACE portfolio also accelerated, with total assessments growing $38 million to $1.3 billion and C-PACE contributing over $27 million in growth.
  • Brown indicated readiness for "significantly" growing the bank, aiming to cross $10 billion in assets and continuing investments in people and technology. She pointed to the bank's track record through challenging environments, noting expansion from $6 billion to nearly $9 billion in assets over five years.
  • Jason Darby, CFO, stated "net income was $26.6 million or $0.88 per diluted share and core net income...was $30 million or $0.99 per diluted share." He explained the quarter included a $41.9 million sale of residential loans resulting in a $3.8 million pretax loss, but noted core earnings benefited from a $1.5 million tax credit. "Net interest income grew by 1.8% to $77.9 million, which exceeded the high end of our guidance range," Darby said, and net interest margin rose to 3.66%.
  • Darby highlighted a dividend increase to $0.17 and buybacks totaling $8.7 million, citing "our confident outlook for 2026 earnings."

Outlook

  • Darby outlined 2026 guidance: "net interest income of $327 million to $331 million or roughly 10% to 11% growth and core pretax pre-provision earnings of $180 million to $183 million or 9% to 10% growth." The bank targets core return on average assets of 1.35%, core return on tangible common equity of 15%, and balance sheet growth of approximately 5%.
  • For Q1 2026, Darby expects net interest income between $79 million and $81 million, and net interest margin to rise, "primarily from increased yields from the loan growth that came on late in the quarter."
  • Technology spending is projected to grow 18%, with annual core OpEx growth to $188 million.
  • Guidance reflects quarterly net loan growth of 1.5% to 2%.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 2025 was $26.6 million or $0.88 per diluted share, with core net income of $30 million or $0.99 per diluted share.
  • Net interest income reached $77.9 million, up 1.8% from the prior quarter, and net interest margin expanded to 3.66%.
  • Core noninterest income was $10.1 million, now representing 11.4% of core revenue. Core expense was $44.9 million, with a core efficiency ratio of 51.13%.
  • Tangible book value per share increased $0.87, and Tier 1 leverage was 9.36%. Buybacks totaled $8.7 million, and the dividend was increased to $0.17 per share.
  • The quarter included a tax credit of $1.5 million and a $3.8 million pretax loss on the sale of residential loans.
  • Asset quality remained solid, though there was a $7.5 million increase in nonaccrual multifamily loans tied to a single D.C. borrower, with reserves increased by $1.9 million.

Q&A

  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler, asked about 2026 provision outlook and effective tax rate. Darby responded that provision is expected "roughly the same from an actual perspective as we've recognized for 2025. Maybe a little bit of improvement there, but I wouldn't, on the margin say it's very significant." He added, "we're targeting to start off a 26.5% ETR."
  • Fitzgibbon inquired about political deposits growth. Brown said "we certainly expect to build through until the election," and Sam Brown noted about 20% quarter-over-quarter political deposit growth, projecting a continued increase until the election.
  • Fitzgibbon asked about multifamily growth. Sam Brown stated "slightly under half of that actually came outside of New York City, which is really good geographic diversification for us."
  • David Konrad, KBW, questioned NIM expansion and loan yields. Darby explained new C&I loans are booked "somewhere in the 5.9% to 6% range...for multifamily and CRE is probably in the 5.70% range," with older real estate loans repricing from the 4.30% range.
  • Konrad also asked about C-PACE growth and portfolio limits. Darby confirmed "no real restriction there in terms of the ability to add assets in a meaningful way," and Sam Brown highlighted that "3/4 of that came from the commercial side."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on credit costs, political deposit growth, and yield trends, with questions often seeking clarification or forward-looking color.
  • Management expressed confidence and optimism, using phrases like "we believe Amalgamated is ready to grow significantly" and "we enter 2026 with clarity, confidence and intent to deliver quality returns on tangible common equity consistently." Tone remained consistent with previous quarters, with an added emphasis on growth and expansion.
  • Compared to Q3, both management and analysts displayed a similar level of engagement, though management's confidence in growth opportunities and balance sheet strength was more pronounced.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Deposit growth accelerated sharply, with nearly $1 billion in new deposits compared to $415 million in Q3, and political deposits rising from $1.4 billion to $1.7 billion.
  • Loan growth in the growth mode portfolios rose 7% versus 3.3% in Q3, and the PACE portfolio also saw a stronger increase.
  • Net interest income and margin continued to rise, with NIM expanding by 6 basis points after a 5 basis point increase in Q3.
  • Management tone shifted from solid performance to a focus on scaling and preparing for asset crossing of $10 billion.
  • Analysts maintained similar areas of focus but probed more deeply into forward-looking provision, political deposit trends, and geographic loan diversification.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited credit turbulence with a D.C. multifamily borrower, resulting in increased reserves and nonaccrual loans, but emphasized the situation is contained and reserves are adequate.
  • Provision expense is expected to remain stable, and management is maintaining a conservative approach given "bumps that we went through in the current year."
  • The bank is monitoring its concentration in C-PACE and traditional securities, shifting balance sheet allocations to maximize yield and diversification.

Final Takeaway

Amalgamated Financial Corp. closed 2025 with record deposit growth, robust loan expansion, and an improved net interest margin, positioning itself to achieve 10%–11% net interest income growth in 2026. The management team remains confident in sustaining momentum through disciplined execution, targeted investments, and resilience amid evolving market conditions, and expects to leverage growth in political deposits and C-PACE portfolios to drive continued performance and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: OFG Bancorp outlines low single-digit loan growth for 2026 while advancing digital strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OFG Bancorp (OFG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • José Fernández, President, CEO & Chairman, reported a 17% year-over-year increase in diluted earnings per share for the quarter on 2% growth in total core revenues, attributing this to disciplined operations and a favorable tax benefit. Asset quality and credit metrics were described as sound, with increased commercial loans and strong acceptance of the Libre and Elite deposit accounts.
  • Fernández highlighted that "one of our biggest strategic and financial accomplishments of 2025 was the progress we made with our Digital First strategy" and described the launch of an omnichannel platform and intelligent banking model that delivers tailored insights to customers.
  • He stated that "capital continued to grow, and we repurchased $40 million of common shares in the fourth quarter," emphasizing robust business activity in Puerto Rico and resilience among businesses and consumers.
  • Maritza Arizmendi, Chief Financial Officer, stated, “Core revenues totaled $185 million, an increase of $1.4 million. Total interest income was $197 million, a decrease of $3 million... Total interest expense was $44 million, a decrease of $1 million... Total banking and financial service revenues were $33 million, an increase of $3.4 million.”
  • Arizmendi noted a loss of $1.1 million in other income due to “$6.1 million for accelerated amortization of technology-related assets,” offset by gains on sales of nonperforming loans and real estate. Noninterest expenses rose $8.5 million, reflecting performance-based advisory costs and business rightsizing.

Outlook

  • Arizmendi projected total noninterest expense for 2026 to be between $380 million to $385 million.
  • She indicated that "loans to continue to grow in low single digits" and expects "retail and commercial deposits to increase with Libre+, Elite, Oriental Biz and our digital offerings driving customer growth."
  • Net interest margin is expected to range between 4.95% to 5.05% in 2026, factoring in funding cost changes and partial exit of government deposits.
  • The effective tax rate for 2026 is estimated around 23%, excluding discrete items.
  • Fernández reiterated a focus on capital return through buybacks, saying "we will continue to be very intentional there. We certainly will also look at the dividend."

Financial Results

  • Core revenues reached $185 million, up $1.4 million sequentially, while total interest income declined $3 million. Total interest expense was $44 million, down $1 million from the third quarter.
  • Total banking and financial service revenues increased by $3.4 million, mainly due to higher wealth management revenues. Other income showed a loss of $1.1 million, reflecting accelerated amortization costs, though partially offset by gains on asset sales.
  • Noninterest expenses totaled $105 million, up $8.5 million, attributed to advisory costs and technology investments.
  • Tangible book value was reported at $29.96 per share, with an efficiency ratio of 56.7%, return on average assets at 1.81%, and return on average tangible common equity at 17.2%.
  • Average loan balances rose slightly, with commercial loans growing to $3.5 billion, representing 43% of the loan book. Core deposit balances were $9.9 billion, up almost 1% from the third quarter.
  • Provision for credit losses was $31.9 million, up $4 million, driven by increased loan volume and specific reserves, notably a Puerto Rico telecommunications commercial loan.

Q&A

  • Kelly Motta, KBW, asked about elevated provisions and charge-offs, with Cesar Ortiz-Marcano, Chief Risk Officer, explaining, “the charge-offs that you're looking in the quarter are the result of a sale that we performed... that released $17 million in nonperforming loans during the quarter,” along with a new nonaccrual telecom loan.
  • Motta also inquired about loan growth outlook; Fernández indicated auto balances are expected to decline 2%-3%, commercial loans to rise 5%-6%, and overall loan growth to remain in low single digits.
  • On expense control, Motta questioned confidence in flat noninterest expenses; Arizmendi emphasized ongoing technology investments and workforce efficiencies, noting, “we are harvesting certain efficiencies... we will start seeing some of that savings and the 2027 and 2028, we see savings to accelerate.”
  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist, asked about 2026 strategic initiatives; Fernández said the focus would shift more to commercial banking and further translating digital strategies to that segment.
  • On capital return, Fernández reiterated the priority of buybacks due to current valuation.
  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde, sought clarity on loan yield and NIM; Arizmendi cited the impact of Fed rate cuts and asset sensitivity as key drivers. Fernández added improved credit quality in auto lending contributed to yield stabilization.
  • Timur Braziler, Wells Fargo, addressed credit normalization; Arizmendi stated a 1% net charge-off rate is likely to persist, while allowance builds included a specific telecom loan reserve.
  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler, explored competitive response to Libre and Elite products; Fernández said, “there's no need to be -- to have a price response because we're not paying high yields... It's not about the rate only. It's about the... value proposition.” He disclosed 75% of Libre accounts are new customers, 40% are 29 years or younger.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern about elevated provisions, loan growth drivers, expense controls, and margin outlook. The tone was probing but not overtly negative, with several follow-ups for clarification on strategy and expense discipline.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently emphasizing asset quality, technology-driven efficiencies, and strategic positioning. Fernández used phrases like "we feel more confident in our expense ranges in '26, for sure" and "we will continue to work hard to bring additional expense reductions."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the overall sentiment remained positive, with increased focus on margin pressures and expense control but sustained confidence from management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s guidance for loan growth shifted from 5%-6% annual growth to "low single digits" for 2026, reflecting heightened caution amid auto loan moderation.
  • Expense guidance for 2026 is more focused on flat year-over-year performance, with a longer-term view toward efficiency gains in 2027-2028.
  • The strategic focus is moving more toward commercial banking, as opposed to prior quarters’ emphasis on retail and digital.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from deposit costs and digital adoption to concerns about provision levels, charge-offs, and the sustainability of expense controls.
  • Management’s confidence in technology-driven efficiencies and capital return remains strong, though there is acknowledgment of competitive pressures and funding cost changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated provision for credit losses, notably due to a specific telecommunications loan, was highlighted as a challenge.
  • Seasonality in auto portfolio charge-offs and delinquencies was discussed, with expectations for normalization in early 2026.
  • Management acknowledged intensifying competition for deposits, especially from U.S. credit unions and targeted CD offerings, but stated their strategy is based on value-added products rather than higher rates.
  • The need to manage the impact of government deposit exits and replacement with higher-cost wholesale funding was noted.
  • Ongoing global macroeconomic and political uncertainties were referenced as factors to monitor for potential impact on Puerto Rico’s economic environment.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored OFG Bancorp’s continued progress in digital banking, robust capital return initiatives, and disciplined expense management. Loan growth is expected in the low single digits for 2026, with a strategic pivot toward commercial banking and technology-led efficiencies. Asset quality remains sound, though elevated provisions related to a single telecom loan and ongoing competitive deposit dynamics will be closely managed in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Capri Holdings gains after TD Cowen says the Michael Kors transformation is working
data.description:
TD Cowen turned bullish on Capri Holdings (CPRI) as it boosted its rating to Buy from Neutral. 

Analyst Oliver Chen and his team pointed to an upgrade to a sharper price point strategy from Capri (CPRI) combined with desirable trend-right styles. The firm also sees substantial operating margin expansion potential this year and financial deleverage opportunities. Progress with the Michael Kors transformation in terms of store closures, outlet remerchandising, handbag pricing architecture, and marketing repositioning is also considered a positive.

It was also highlighted that the new Michael Kors products appear to be on trend, and the company is embracing data to drive personalization.

TD Cowen raised its price target on Capri (CPRI) to $32 from $25 based on a 16X multiple to the two-year out earnings estimate. "We are encouraged by improved product at Michael Kors, strategic pricing architecture aimed to increase full-price sell-through and average unit retail, store rationalization, and outlet elevation effort," wrote Chen.

Shares of Capri Holdings (CPRI) were up 2.9% in Thursday afternoon trading.
